text.title: Help and Documentation — Core Rules
text.summary: Concrete rules for implementing Nielsen Heuristic #10: layered help hierarchy, contextual placement, task-focused content, and maintenance discipline.

rule.statement: Follow the help hierarchy in order: interface design first (no help needed), then inline hints, tooltips, guided tours, FAQs, documentation, and human support as a last resort.
rule.rationale: Each layer up the hierarchy adds friction and cost. If a tooltip can answer the question, a knowledge base article is overkill.

rule.statement: If a UI element requires a tooltip to be understood, fix the UI first — help content cannot substitute for clear design.
rule.rationale: Documentation that compensates for poor design grows unbounded and becomes impossible to maintain as the UI evolves.

rule.statement: Place help at the point of confusion: inline hints below inputs, tooltips on ? icons, contextual sidebars on complex pages.
rule.rationale: Help content found only in a separate help center requires users to leave their task context, increasing cognitive load and abandonment risk.

rule.statement: Every empty state is help content — design empty states with action guidance, not just "No items found."
rule.rationale: Empty states are the most frequent onboarding moment; they are the first help interaction most users encounter.

rule.statement: Write help content in user language (support ticket language), not product-team language — users search with their words, not yours.
rule.rationale: Search success rate directly correlates with vocabulary alignment between help content and user mental models.

rule.statement: Every how-to article must have a "Result" section describing what success looks like after completing all steps.
rule.rationale: Users who complete steps but do not know if they succeeded will contact support anyway; a clear success description closes the loop.

rule.statement: Keep inline hints under 15 words; tooltips under 40 words; how-to articles structured with numbered steps and no more than 7 steps per section.
rule.rationale: Users in an error state or onboarding context have limited attention; long help content is skipped.

rule.statement: Write help content at the moment a feature ships — treat it as a definition of done, not a post-launch task.
rule.rationale: Retroactive documentation is always incomplete; features ship faster than writers can reconstruct intent from memory.

rule.statement: Version-pin screenshots with the app version they were taken from; stale screenshots erode trust faster than missing ones.
rule.rationale: A screenshot showing a UI that no longer exists actively misleads users and generates support tickets.

rule.statement: Track "zero-results help searches" weekly — each is an explicit content gap signal.
rule.rationale: Search analytics reveal user intent vocabulary that no content strategy meeting can surface.

text.reference: Script to check docs for broken links and stale content markers
